purpose: Explain what signal flow is, why it exists, and how it behaves, using plain language for someone with no prior exposure.
assumptions: The reader has never heard the term signal flow
The reader does not know TouchDesigner
The reader does not know programming concepts
The reader understands time, change, and cause-and-effect

core_concept.name: Signal Flow
core_concept.definition: Signal flow is the idea that something changes over time and passes through steps that affect it.
why_it_exists: Because things change instead of staying frozen
Because one change often causes another
Because complex behavior is easier to understand as a sequence than as a whole

flow_laws.meaning: What happens earlier can affect what happens later.
Influence goes one way, from source to result.

exceptions: Generators, which create something on their own

flow_laws.law: Flow does not remember by default
flow_laws.meaning: Each moment is treated as new unless something is explicitly stored.

glossary.definition: Anything that can change over time and affect something else.
The ongoing movement of a signal through time and influence.
examples: A picture becoming brighter frame by frame

glossary.definition: What the signal is like at a single moment.
A difference between one moment and the next.
The signal stays the same thing but appears differently.
The signal’s character is altered.
Something exists where nothing existed before.
Some parts of the signal are reduced or removed.
Multiple signals influence one result.
One signal produces multiple related results.
examples: Same signal viewed in multiple ways

glossary.definition: A point where behavior changes.
A brief event that happens and then disappears.

mental_model.summary: Signal flow is a way of thinking about change over time.
mental_model.core_question: What goes in, what changes, what comes out?
why_this_matters: It lets you reason without menus

band_goal: Establish what a signal is, what flow means, and how to think about change before any tools, screens, or systems exist.

entries.definition: Anything that can change over time and influence something else.
entries.intuition: If it can be different now than it was a moment ago, it counts.

entries.definition: The forward movement of a signal through time.
entries.intuition: One moment leads into the next.

entries.definition: The ordering of moments that allows change to exist.
entries.intuition: Without time, nothing could be different.
not_this: Not a unit you must measure

entries.definition: A single slice of time.
entries.intuition: One instant, taken alone.

entries.definition: What a signal is like at one moment.
entries.intuition: A snapshot of how things stand right now.

entries.definition: A difference between one moment and the next.
entries.intuition: Something did not stay the same.
not_this: Not motion in space by default

entries.definition: Where a signal begins.
entries.intuition: The place change first appears.

entries.definition: What exists after the signal has flowed and changed.
entries.intuition: What you end up seeing or using.

entries.definition: When one signal affects another without replacing it.
entries.intuition: A nudge rather than a takeover.

entries.definition: Whether change feels connected from moment to moment.
entries.intuition: Does it feel like one ongoing thing or separate steps?

exit_condition: After this band, the reader understands that signal flow is about time, change, and influence—nothing more.

band_goal: Explain how signals evolve, move, and differ over time, without assuming space, visuals, or mechanics.

entries.definition: The amount a signal differs from one moment to the next.
entries.intuition: How much it refuses to stay the same.

entries.definition: How quickly change happens over time.
entries.intuition: Fast-changing versus slow-changing.

entries.definition: The tendency of change to move one way rather than another.
entries.intuition: Upward, downward, increasing, decreasing.

entries.definition: The tendency of a signal to keep changing in the same way.
entries.intuition: Once it’s going, it keeps going.

entries.definition: Change that moves back and forth between states.
entries.intuition: Repeating swings.

entries.definition: The span between the smallest and largest states a signal reaches.
entries.intuition: How far it is allowed to go.

entries.definition: Slow, accumulated change over time.
entries.intuition: It wanders without a sharp push.

entries.definition: Change that happens in jumps rather than continuously.
entries.intuition: One state, then suddenly another.

entries.definition: Resistance to change over time.
entries.intuition: It stays put unless pushed.

entries.definition: The passage from one state to another.
entries.intuition: How change is crossed, not just where it starts or ends.

exit_condition: After this band, the reader can describe how a signal changes over time without referring to tools, space, or implementation.

band_goal: Explain how signals cause moments, switches, and conditional behavior without implying intention, logic, or planning.

entries.definition: Something that happens at a specific moment.
entries.intuition: A point in time where behavior changes or is noticed.

entries.definition: An event that causes something else to happen.
entries.intuition: This happens, so that happens.

entries.definition: A boundary where behavior changes when crossed.
entries.intuition: Below this, one thing happens. Above it, another.

entries.definition: A requirement that must be met for something to occur.
entries.intuition: Only when this is true.

entries.definition: A point where flow is allowed or blocked.
entries.intuition: Open or closed.

entries.definition: A brief signal that exists for a short moment.
entries.intuition: It appears, then disappears.

entries.definition: The instant when a signal changes state.
entries.intuition: The exact moment of change.

entries.definition: Evaluating one signal against another or against a reference.
entries.intuition: Is this bigger, smaller, or the same?

entries.definition: Choosing which signal continues forward.
entries.intuition: This one goes on, that one does not.

entries.definition: A change that redirects flow from one path to another.
entries.intuition: Flow is rerouted.

exit_condition: After this band, the reader can describe how moments and conditions alter flow without imagining logic, thought, or agency.

band_goal: Explain how multiple signals affect each other without implying hierarchy, ownership, or control.

entries.definition: When two or more signals affect the same outcome.
entries.intuition: They meet and the result reflects both.

entries.definition: When one signal determines the behavior of another.
entries.intuition: This one decides how that one behaves.
examples: A signal deciding when something happens

entries.definition: Using one signal to continuously shape another.
entries.intuition: One signal bends the behavior of another over time.

entries.definition: One signal replaces another when active.
entries.intuition: When this is present, the other no longer matters.

entries.definition: Signals contribute together to form a combined result.
entries.intuition: Each one adds something to the outcome.

entries.definition: How strongly a signal contributes relative to others.
entries.intuition: Some voices count more than others.

entries.definition: When one signal cannot exist meaningfully without another.
entries.intuition: This only matters because that exists.

entries.definition: When changes in one signal are mirrored by another.
entries.intuition: They move together.

entries.definition: Preventing one signal from affecting another.
entries.intuition: This change does not leak over.

entries.definition: Where and how far a signal’s influence applies.
entries.intuition: What it reaches and what it does not.

exit_condition: After this band, the reader understands how multiple signals coexist, shape each other, or stay separate without imagining intent or logic.

band_goal: Explain what persists, what does not, and how time affects signals without implying awareness, storage devices, or planning.

entries.definition: Whether a signal continues to exist beyond the current moment.
entries.intuition: Does it stick around or vanish immediately?

entries.definition: The ability to retain past states and use them later.
entries.intuition: What happened before can matter now.

entries.definition: The sequence of past states a signal has gone through.
entries.intuition: The trail behind the current moment.

entries.definition: A shift in time between cause and effect.
entries.intuition: It happens later than expected.

entries.definition: Change that builds up over time.
entries.intuition: Each moment adds to the last.

entries.definition: Gradual loss of influence over time.
entries.intuition: It fades instead of stopping suddenly.

entries.definition: Returning a signal to an initial or neutral state.
entries.intuition: Back to the beginning.

entries.definition: The time it takes for a change to be observed after it occurs.
entries.intuition: The wait before you see it.

entries.definition: Observing a signal at specific moments rather than continuously.
entries.intuition: Checking in at intervals.

entries.definition: Whether a signal carries forward unchanged when nothing acts on it.
entries.intuition: Does it keep going as-is?

exit_condition: After this band, the reader understands what aspects of a signal persist across time, what must be stored deliberately, and why past moments do not matter unless explicitly carried forward.

band_goal: Explain what it means when nothing happens, why expectations fail, and where signals stop mattering.

entries.definition: When no signal is present at a given moment.
entries.intuition: There is nothing to act on.

entries.definition: A signal that exists but has no magnitude.
entries.intuition: Something is there, but it has no strength.

entries.definition: A state where no noticeable change occurs.
entries.intuition: Nothing seems to be happening.

entries.definition: The tendency of a signal to remain unchanged unless acted upon.
entries.intuition: It keeps doing what it was doing.

entries.definition: A boundary beyond which a signal cannot go.
entries.intuition: This is as far as it gets.

entries.definition: When increasing input no longer changes the output.
entries.intuition: More effort has no effect.

entries.definition: Forcing a signal to stop at a boundary.
entries.intuition: It hits a wall.

entries.definition: What happens when expected input is missing.
entries.intuition: What it does instead.

entries.definition: When a signal cannot produce a meaningful result.
entries.intuition: The flow stops making sense.

entries.definition: A mismatch between what is assumed and what flow actually does.
entries.intuition: You expected something to happen, but nothing did.

exit_condition: After this band, the reader understands why nothing happening is often correct behavior, and where signals naturally stop or lose influence.

goal: Unify all six bands into a single coherent mental model with consistent language, clear progression, and no conceptual gaps.

language_constraints: No implication of intent, intelligence, or agency
global_rules.reading_order: Linear, but each band must stand alone
global_rules.core_question_everywhere: What goes in, what changes, what comes out, what does not happen?

conceptual_spine.connects_to_next: Once something exists and flows, it must change.

conceptual_spine.role: Describe how things evolve over time.
conceptual_spine.connects_to_next: Once change exists, moments can matter.

conceptual_spine.role: Explain when change causes noticeable moments.
conceptual_spine.connects_to_next: Once moments exist, signals can affect each other.
Once signals interact, persistence becomes relevant.

conceptual_spine.role: Explain what carries forward and what does not.
conceptual_spine.connects_to_next: Once persistence is understood, absence becomes meaningful.

conceptual_spine.role: Explain why nothing happening is often correct.
conceptual_spine.connects_to_next: No further band; this resolves expectations.

signal.consistency_note: Always means something that can change over time and influence something else.

change.consistency_note: Never implies improvement or spatial motion.

influence.consistency_note: Influence shapes; it does not decide.

absence.consistency_note: Means nothing is present, not that nothing exists.

cross_band_clarifications.resolution: Stability describes resistance to change; persistence describes survival across moments.
A trigger is a moment; a transition is the passage between states.
Control shapes behavior continuously; override replaces behavior when active.
Zero is something with no magnitude; absence is nothing at all.
Delay is intentional separation in time; latency is inherent waiting before observation.

after_reading: The reader can describe any TouchDesigner system without naming tools
The reader understands why nothing happening is often correct
The reader does not expect memory unless it is introduced
The reader can predict behavior by reasoning about flow
what_the_reader_will_not_do: Assume visual motion equals signal change

authoring_rules_for_future_layers.layer_2_dependency: All Layer 2 operator stories must use only glossary terms from this document.
authoring_rules_for_future_layers.violation_test: If a sentence cannot be explained using glossary terms, it does not belong in Layer 2.
authoring_rules_for_future_layers.version_safety: Any sentence here should remain true across future versions.

intent: Present signal flow as a single, continuous idea rather than six separate sections. This version removes band boundaries as a learning requirement while preserving them as an internal structure.

how_to_read.instruction: Read top to bottom once. After that, treat this as a reference.
how_to_read.mental_posture: Assume nothing is trying to be smart. Nothing wants anything. Things only change when acted on.

conceptual_arc.middle: That change moves through time, sometimes causing moments, sometimes interacting with other change.
conceptual_arc.end: If nothing happens, that is often the correct result.

glossary.definition: Anything that can change over time and influence something else.
The forward progression of a signal through time.
The ordering of moments that allows change to exist.
A single slice of time.
What a signal is like at one moment.
A difference between one moment and the next.
How much a signal differs from moment to moment.
How quickly change happens over time.
The tendency of change to move one way rather than another.
The tendency of change to continue once it has begun.
Change that moves back and forth between states.
The passage from one state to another.
Something that happens at a specific moment.
An event that causes something else to happen.
A boundary where behavior changes when crossed.
A point where flow is allowed or blocked.
A brief signal that exists for a short moment.
When one signal affects another without replacing it.
Multiple signals affecting the same outcome.
One signal determining how another behaves.
Signals contributing together to form a result.
When one signal only matters because another exists.
Whether a signal continues beyond the current moment.
The ability for past states to influence the present.
The sequence of past states a signal has passed through.
A separation in time between cause and effect.
Returning a signal to a neutral or initial state.
No signal is present.
A signal that exists but has no magnitude.
No noticeable change occurs.
A boundary beyond which a signal cannot go.
Assuming something should happen when flow says it should not.

unifying_rules: Nothing remembers unless memory is introduced.
Nothing moves unless change is present.
Nothing happens is often the correct outcome.
Signals influence; they do not decide.
Flow explains behavior without explaining implementation.

purpose: Describe families of operators by what role they play in signal flow, using only Layer 2 vocabulary. No tools, menus, or implementation details.
reading_rule: Each family answers: what goes in, what changes, what comes out, what does not happen.

families.role: Create a signal where none existed.
what_comes_out: A signal with variation over time
what_does_not_happen: They do not react to downstream results
They do not remember unless memory is added
common_expectation_traps: Assuming generators respond to later changes

families.role: Change how a signal appears without changing what it is.
what_changes: Position, orientation, or scale in a conceptual sense

families.role: Alter the character of a signal.
what_comes_out: A signal that feels different but remains continuous
what_does_not_happen: They do not replace the signal

families.role: Reduce or emphasize parts of a signal.
what_changes: Some aspects are reduced or removed

families.role: Allow multiple signals to influence one result.
what_changes: Signals are blended, averaged, or layered
what_does_not_happen: Signals do not become aware of each other

families.role: Detect moments that cause behavior to change.
Allow past states to influence the present.
what_does_not_happen: They do not predict the future

families.role: Shift when things are observed or acted upon.
what_comes_out: The same signal, later or offset
what_does_not_happen: They do not smooth by default

layer_3_rules: Every operator belongs to exactly one primary family
If an operator seems to belong to multiple families, its signal story is unclear
Layer 3 must only use Layer 2 terms
exit_condition: The reader can classify any operator by role before learning its details.

purpose: Surface the most common incorrect assumptions people bring to each operator family, stated plainly so they can be unlearned early.
scope_rule: Traps describe expectations, not errors. Nothing here implies misuse or failure.

expectation_traps.assumption: Generators react to what comes later.
expectation_traps.reality: They begin flow; they do not look downstream.
Generation can be stable, repeating, or guided.
They start fresh unless memory is added.
Guiding influence can exist without a primary input.
They only change presentation, not substance.
Without change over time, nothing moves.
Identity stays intact.
They alter character while preserving continuity.
They act only on what arrives now.
More influence can erase nuance.
Emphasis always removes something else.
Order and structure remain the same.
They only reduce or emphasize what exists.
Influences combine; histories stay separate.
Some signals can dominate.
Crowding can flatten outcomes.
Selection is discrete unless transition is added.
Each moment is evaluated independently.
Only the selected path proceeds.
They occur and disappear.
They only mark moments.
Each trigger stands alone.
They pass or block without modification.
Blocked flow is absent, not delayed.
They only allow or prevent passage.
Memory must be explicitly introduced.
Stored history influences forward flow only.
Only retained history has influence.
Only when content is experienced changes.
Delay shifts time, not character.
Latency is inherent waiting.

authoring_rule: If an operator breaks one of these expectations, call it out explicitly in its Layer 2 story.
